past_medical_history: Anxiety disorder diagnosed at age 28, managed with sertraline
Migraine without aura, approximately 2-3 episodes per month since adolescence
No history of febrile seizures or CNS infection
family_history: Mother with type 2 diabetes and hypothyroidism
Younger brother with childhood absence epilepsy, outgrown by age 14
No family history of temporal lobe epilepsy or structural brain disease

social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine with dinner, approximately 2-3 glasses per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a second-floor apartment, no children
social_history.exercise: Runs 3-4 times per week, trains for half-marathons
social_history.driving: Drives daily to work, approximately 25-minute commute

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech fluent with normal prosody. Naming and repetition intact. Recalls 3/3 words at five minutes. Attention intact on serial sevens. However, during the interview the patient paused twice for approximately 3-4 seconds with a momentary blank stare, after which she resumed conversation seamlessly, appearing unaware of the interruptions.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations. No visual field deficits on confrontation testing.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone throughout. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups tested bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift. No involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception intact in all extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin without dysmetria bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait with appropriate arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue bite marks. No signs of recent injury. Subtle olfactory identification difficulty on informal bedside testing -- correctly identified 2 of 5 common odors (coffee and cinnamon identified; peppermint, lemon, and vanilla missed), though patient attributed this to chronic nasal congestion.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of suddenly feeling detached from surroundings with an odd smell, followed by confusion and inability to recall the episodes
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Chen is a 41-year-old right-handed woman and middle school art teacher who was brought to the emergency department by a colleague after a witnessed episode of unresponsiveness at work. Her colleague reports that during a faculty meeting, Ms. Chen suddenly stopped talking mid-sentence, appeared to stare blankly, and then began repetitively picking at her blouse with her left hand while making subtle chewing motions with her mouth. The colleague called her name several times but received no response. The episode lasted approximately 60 to 90 seconds, after which Ms. Chen appeared confused and disoriented for roughly five minutes. She had no memory of the event and was surprised when told what had happened.

Upon further questioning, Ms. Chen reports that over the past eight months she has experienced intermittent episodes of suddenly perceiving an unpleasant, hard-to-describe metallic or burnt smell that she knows is not actually present, followed by a sensation of being disconnected from her surroundings, as though she were watching herself from outside her body. These episodes are brief, lasting 20 to 40 seconds, and she has remained aware during most of them, though occasionally she notices gaps in her memory afterward. She has experienced these episodes approximately two to three times per month and initially attributed them to anxiety or stress at work. She reports that on two occasions in the past three months she woke up in the morning with a bitten tongue and significant muscle soreness, though she has no memory of nocturnal events. She has also noticed increasing difficulty remembering students' names, which she finds unusual and distressing given her previously excellent memory. She denies any recent head trauma, illness, or medication changes. Her migraines have continued at their usual frequency and are phenomenologically different from these new episodes. She has never seen a neurologist for these symptoms and has not had any prior EEG or brain imaging.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.symmetry: Grossly symmetric, though intermittent subtle amplitude asymmetry noted with slightly lower amplitude over the right temporal region -- clinical significance uncertain
background.sleep_features: Stage II sleep achieved with normal vertex waves and sleep spindles

findings.frequency: Rare -- 4 discharges captured over a 45-minute recording, occurring during drowsiness
findings.morphology: Biphasic sharp transients with possible phase reversal at F8, duration 50-80 ms; morphology is somewhat atypical and does not definitively meet criteria for epileptiform discharges versus benign variants (wicket spikes)
findings.state: Drowsiness transitioning to stage I sleep

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs of 3-4 Hz polymorphic delta activity, each lasting 2-5 seconds, seen approximately every 5-8 minutes
findings.morphology: Irregular polymorphic delta, not clearly rhythmic

artifacts.impact: Moderate intermittent contamination of temporal channels, partly obscuring morphological analysis of the sharp transients
Brief, easily distinguished from cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes of hyperventilation produced generalized background slowing without asymmetry or provocation of epileptiform discharges
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at multiple frequencies. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG. Rare right anterior-mid temporal sharp transients of uncertain significance are noted during drowsiness. These may represent epileptiform activity localizing to the right temporal region, but their morphology overlaps with wicket spikes, a benign variant, and definitive classification is not possible on this limited recording. Additionally, intermittent right temporal polymorphic delta slowing is present, which is nonspecific but may suggest underlying right temporal pathology. No electrographic seizures captured. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording with significant muscle artifact over temporal channels during portions of wakefulness. No clinical events captured during the study. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring is strongly recommended to resolve the ambiguity of the sharp transients and to capture habitual clinical events.

findings.size: Right hippocampal volume marginally smaller than left on visual inspection; formal volumetrics pending

signal_characteristics.T1: Slight loss of normal internal architecture of the right hippocampus compared to left, most apparent on thin coronal sections
signal_characteristics.T2: Equivocal increased signal in the right hippocampal body; findings are subtle and could represent partial volume artifact versus early pathological change
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Questionable subtle hyperintensity in the right hippocampal body on coronal FLAIR images; not definitively abnormal

additional_observations: Small left frontal white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensity is nonspecific and most likely represents a small vessel ischemic change or perivascular space, though clinical correlation is suggested
mri.impression: Equivocal subtle asymmetry of the hippocampi with the right appearing marginally smaller and showing questionable T2/FLAIR signal change. A 3 mm nonspecific left frontal white matter lesion is noted as an incidental finding. Clinical correlation recommended.

background.symmetry: Mild persistent right temporal amplitude attenuation compared to left, now more clearly apparent over 48 hours of recording
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes

findings.frequency: Occasional, averaging 3-4 per hour, significantly activated during NREM sleep where frequency increases to 8-10 per hour
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8 with field extending to T4 and occasionally F4, duration 50-70 ms with clear aftergoing slow wave. In sleep, morphology is definitively epileptiform, distinguishable from wicket spikes by presence of aftergoing slow wave and disruption of background
findings.state: Predominantly during NREM sleep, rare in drowsiness, not seen in alert wakefulness

findings.location: Right temporal onset (F8/T4 with rapid spread to T4/T6)
findings.frequency: One clinical seizure captured during 48-hour monitoring
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with right temporal low-amplitude fast activity (beta range) evolving to rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta over 10 seconds, then progressing to 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta over right temporal region lasting 48 seconds total before offset
findings.state: Occurred during wakefulness while patient was watching television

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs, more frequent than on routine EEG
findings.morphology: Polymorphic delta activity, 2-3 Hz, with some runs showing a semi-rhythmic quality approaching TIRDA morphology

artifacts.impact: Brief obscuration during onset of automatisms, but seizure onset clearly captured before artifact began

output.impression: Abnormal 48-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. One habitual clinical seizure captured with unequivocal right anterior temporal electrographic onset concordant with clinical semiology (olfactory aura, behavioral arrest, contralateral manual automatisms, oral automatisms, post-ictal confusion with dysphasia). Interictal right anterior temporal sharp waves are confirmed as epileptiform, particularly during NREM sleep where morphology is definitive. Intermittent right temporal slowing persists. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Only one seizure captured during 48-hour recording. Additional seizures would strengthen lateralization confidence.

findings.location: Right hippocampus, body and head predominantly affected
findings.size: Right hippocampal volume 2.4 cm3 versus left 3.3 cm3

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Subtle loss of internal digitations of the right hippocampal head, reduced gray-white contrast compared to left
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Definite hyperintense signal in the right hippocampal body on thin coronal cuts (2 mm slices), now clearly distinguishable from left on optimized sequences
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Hyperintense signal in the right hippocampal body and head, confirmed abnormal with comparison to contralateral side

findings.mass_effect: None. Borderline prominence of right temporal horn compared to left.
findings.borders: Atrophic changes without discrete mass lesion

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 2.4 cm3 (8th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.left_hippocampus: 3.3 cm3 (42nd percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: Automated hippocampal volumetry confirms right hippocampal volume loss
Left frontal white matter lesion unchanged from prior study, considered incidental
output.impression: No dual pathology or additional epileptogenic lesions identified. Incidental left frontal white matter lesion is stable and nonspecific. Clinical correlation recommended.

Neuropsychological_Testing.test: Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Visual Memory (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 35 T-score (H), Visual Memory (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 32 T-score (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Visual Memory (BVMT-R Total Learning): T-score 35 T-score (L)
Visual Memory (BVMT-R Delayed Recall): T-score 32 T-score (H)

findings.location: Right mesial and lateral temporal lobe

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Moderately reduced FDG uptake in the right mesial temporal structures (hippocampus, parahippocampal gyrus) and mildly reduced uptake in the right lateral temporal cortex relative to the left
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Right-to-left temporal lobe asymmetry index 0.82 (normal >0.90), indicating significant right temporal hypometabolism

findings.location: Bilateral frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes; left temporal lobe; basal ganglia; thalami; cerebellum

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET acquired during continuous EEG monitoring confirming interictal state
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates right mesial and lateral temporal hypometabolism, concordant with right mesial temporal sclerosis on 3T MRI and right temporal epileptiform activity on video-EEG. Multi-modal concordance strongly supports right temporal lobe epilepsy. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: temporal lobe epilepsy olfactory aura uncinate seizures lateralization

results.title: Olfactory auras in focal epilepsy: clinical, EEG and MRI correlates
results.summary: Olfactory auras (uncinate fits) are associated with mesial temporal lobe epilepsy. They occur in approximately 5-10% of temporal lobe epilepsy patients and may lateralize to either hemisphere. Unpleasant olfactory hallucinations are more common than pleasant ones.

results.title: Lateralizing value of seizure semiology in temporal lobe epilepsy
results.summary: Contralateral manual automatisms (hand fumbling ipsilateral to seizure onset) and ipsilateral dystonic posturing are reliable lateralizing signs. Olfactory auras alone have limited lateralizing value but combined with other signs improve localization.

results.title: Subtle MRI-negative temporal lobe epilepsy: role of 3T MRI and FDG-PET
results.summary: Approximately 20-30% of temporal lobe epilepsy patients have normal or equivocal 1.5T MRI findings. 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol detects subtle hippocampal sclerosis missed on standard imaging in a significant proportion. FDG-PET adds complementary lateralizing information.

output.summary: Olfactory auras (uncinate seizures) localize to the mesial temporal lobe, particularly the amygdala and uncus, but have limited lateralizing value on their own. Contralateral hand automatisms (left hand in this case) lateralize to the right temporal lobe. Up to 30% of temporal lobe epilepsy cases are MRI-negative at 1.5T, underscoring the importance of 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol. Multi-modal concordance (semiology, EEG, MRI, PET) is the cornerstone of pre-surgical evaluation.

output.proposed: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily, titrate to 200 mg daily over 8 weeks
interactions: Sertraline: No clinically significant pharmacokinetic interaction. Both are well tolerated in combination. Sertraline does not significantly affect lamotrigine metabolism.
contraindications: Penicillin allergy (urticaria) documented -- no cross-reactivity with lamotrigine, but heightened vigilance for rash is warranted in patients with drug allergy history
warnings: Risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis, especially with rapid titration. Must use slow titration schedule.
Any rash during titration should prompt immediate evaluation and consideration of discontinuation
Not teratogenic category D but associated with cleft palate risk at higher doses -- relevant for this woman of childbearing age, counsel on contraception
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, titrate to 1000-1500 mg twice daily (faster onset of therapeutic effect but risk of psychiatric side effects, relevant given anxiety disorder history)
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 200 mg twice daily
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg twice daily, titrate to 600 mg twice daily (monitor sodium levels)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (right mesial temporal lobe epilepsy with hippocampal sclerosis)

differential.key_features: History of anxiety disorder could suggest psychogenic etiology. However, stereotyped semiology with olfactory aura, contralateral automatisms, and post-ictal confusion with amnesia is highly suggestive of epileptic seizures. EEG confirmation of ictal correlate excludes PNES for the captured event.

Patient has a known history of migraines, which could initially confuse the picture. However, her migraines are without aura and phenomenologically distinct from these episodes. Migraine aura spreads slowly over minutes, while her olfactory episodes are abrupt and followed by altered awareness and automatisms, not headache.

Age 41 without vascular risk factors. TIAs produce negative symptoms and do not typically cause olfactory hallucinations, automatisms, or post-ictal confusion. Episodes are recurrent and stereotyped, not consistent with TIA.
No prodrome of lightheadedness, pallor, or diaphoresis. Prolonged post-ictal confusion and automatisms during the event are incompatible with simple syncope. No postural trigger.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history of episode semiology from patient and witnesses, including aura description, automatisms, duration, and post-ictal features
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Olfactory aura (unpleasant metallic smell), depersonalization, behavioral arrest with contralateral hand automatisms and oral automatisms, post-ictal confusion with amnesia

optimal_actions.action: Perform complete neurological examination including bedside olfactory testing and mental status assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Largely normal exam with subtle olfactory identification deficit and possible brief behavioral pauses during interview suggesting subclinical seizure activity
Equivocal right temporal sharp transients and intermittent right temporal slowing; findings are ambiguous and require further monitoring
Equivocal subtle right hippocampal asymmetry on 1.5T; recommend 3T epilepsy protocol

optimal_actions.action: Order basic labs including BMP, CBC, prolactin, TSH, and urine drug screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel; mildly elevated prolactin confounded by late sampling and sertraline; negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Request prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual events and resolve equivocal routine EEG findings
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Capture of habitual seizure with right temporal onset confirming epilepsy diagnosis and lateralization

optimal_actions.action: Request 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol given equivocal 1.5T findings
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmed right mesial temporal sclerosis with hippocampal volume loss and T2/FLAIR hyperintensity

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy appropriate for focal epilepsy, checking for drug interactions with current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lamotrigine or levetiracetam are appropriate first-line options; consideration of psychiatric side effects of levetiracetam given anxiety history

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient on driving restrictions, seizure safety, and the importance of medication adherence
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient informed of state driving restrictions, avoidance of heights and unsupervised swimming, and seizure first aid for household members

optimal_actions.action: Refer for neuropsychological testing and epilepsy surgery evaluation at a comprehensive epilepsy center
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Referral placed given structural lesion and potential surgical candidacy

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to identify epileptiform activity; pursue prolonged video-EEG when routine EEG is equivocal
Obtain brain MRI; escalate to 3T epilepsy protocol when 1.5T findings are inconclusive
Obtain basic metabolic panel and CBC to exclude metabolic causes of seizures
Initiate appropriate antiepileptic drug therapy for focal epilepsy
Counsel patient on driving restrictions and seizure safety precautions
Recognize that the olfactory aura with depersonalization and subsequent automatisms represent temporal lobe seizure semiology, not anxiety or panic attacks
contraindicated_actions: Dismissing the episodes as panic attacks or anxiety without obtaining EEG and MRI
Performing lumbar puncture without clinical indication (no signs of infection or inflammation)
Prescribing bupropion which lowers seizure threshold
Discharging patient without AED initiation or neurology follow-up after confirmed seizure
Advising the patient that she may continue driving without restriction
Rapidly titrating lamotrigine (risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome)
Attributing the left frontal white matter lesion to the seizure etiology and pursuing unnecessary workup for demyelinating disease
key_reasoning_points: Olfactory hallucinations (uncinate fits) localize seizure onset to the mesial temporal lobe, specifically the amygdala and uncinate region, though they do not strongly lateralize
Contralateral (left) hand automatisms during the witnessed seizure are a lateralizing sign pointing to right temporal lobe seizure onset
The subtle behavioral pauses observed during the neurological exam may represent brief focal impaired awareness seizures, underscoring the need for video-EEG monitoring

Equivocal sharp transients on routine EEG that overlap with wicket spikes (a benign variant) require prolonged monitoring for definitive classification -- this is a key moderate-difficulty diagnostic challenge
A 1.5T MRI may miss subtle hippocampal sclerosis that becomes apparent on 3T with epilepsy-specific sequences -- recognizing the need to escalate imaging is essential
Prolactin elevation must be interpreted cautiously when sampling is delayed and the patient is on an SSRI, both of which confound interpretation
The pre-existing anxiety diagnosis and migraine history create diagnostic noise that could lead an unwary clinician to attribute temporal lobe seizure symptoms to psychiatric or migraine etiologies
The incidental left frontal white matter lesion is a red herring that should not be pursued as the seizure focus, given right-sided lateralization of all other findings

metadata.estimated_complexity: Moderate - equivocal initial EEG and MRI findings requiring escalation, confounding psychiatric history and medication effects
